title.emphasis: Section III: A First Amendment Analysis of Prostitution

section.para: This section applies the legal frameworks established in Sections I and II directly to the communicative and physical acts of prostitution. By dissecting the transaction into its constituent parts, it becomes clear that neither component qualifies for First Amendment protection under existing constitutional doctrine.

title.emphasis: 3.1 Deconstructing the Transaction: Speech and Conduct Components

section.para: The act of prostitution is not a monolithic event but a process that involves distinct elements. A comprehensive legal analysis must address each one separately:

listitem.para: (a) The Communicative Acts: This includes the solicitation of a client, the negotiation of terms (services to be rendered, price), and the final agreement to engage in the transaction. These are fundamentally acts of speech.14
(b) The Physical Conduct: This is the commercial exchange of a sexual act for money or other consideration. This is the underlying conduct that the speech proposes and facilitates.14

title.emphasis: 3.2 The Speech of Prostitution: An Unprotected Category

section.para: The communicative acts associated with prostitution fail to gain First Amendment protection under at least two independent and dispositive doctrines.

title.emphasis: Analysis as Commercial Speech Proposing an Illegal Transaction

section.para: As established in Section I, the Central Hudson test for commercial speech contains a critical threshold requirement: the speech must concern "lawful activity".13 Prostitution is illegal in nearly every U.S. jurisdiction, with the limited exception of licensed brothels in some rural counties of Nevada.5 Therefore, speech soliciting or proposing an act of prostitution inherently concerns unlawful activity. This places it squarely outside the scope of protected commercial speech.

The Ninth Circuit's reasoning in Erotic Service Provider Legal, Education & Research Project (ESP) v. GASCON (2018) is directly on point. In that case, the court reviewed a First Amendment challenge to California's law criminalizing prostitution. The court began and ended its commercial speech analysis with the first prong of the Central Hudson test. Because prostitution is an unlawful activity in California, the court held, the speech soliciting it is not protected, and the First Amendment analysis ends there.14 The illegality of the underlying conduct is constitutionally fatal to any claim of protection for the speech that proposes it.

title.emphasis: Analysis as Speech Integral to the Crime of Prostitution

section.para: Even if viewed outside the commercial speech context, the words of solicitation and agreement are the direct instruments of a crime. In most jurisdictions, the verbal offer and acceptance constitute the crime of solicitation or conspiracy to commit prostitution.14 The speech is not a commentary on prostitution or an abstract discussion of its merits; it is the

commission of a crime related to prostitution. As such, it falls squarely within the "speech integral to criminal conduct" exception and receives no First Amendment protection.9 The words are the means by which the illegal transaction is initiated and formed, making them inseparable from the criminal conduct itself.

title.emphasis: 3.3 The Conduct of Prostitution: A Lack of Expressive Content

section.para: The physical act of commercial sex also fails to qualify for First Amendment protection as expressive conduct. It is legally distinct from other forms of commercialized sexuality, such as nude dancing or pornography, which are considered expressive because they are created for an audience.

title.emphasis: Failure to Meet the Spence Test

section.para: Unlike nude dancing, which is a performance intended to convey an erotic message to an audience, the act of prostitution is a private, commercial transaction.24 It is not a performance intended to convey a "particularized message" to the public in a way that would satisfy the

Spence test.2 While one could posit that the act expresses ideas about sexuality, commerce, or human relationships, there is no "great likelihood" that such a message would be understood by a viewing public in the way a protest, a play, or a dance is. The conduct is not "inherently expressive" because its primary purpose is transactional, not communicative.2

title.emphasis: The Decisive Precedent of Arcara v. Cloud Books, Inc. (1986)

section.para: The Supreme Court provided its most direct statement on this matter in Arcara v. Cloud Books, Inc. The case involved the closure of an adult bookstore under a public health nuisance law because solicitation of prostitution and other illicit sexual activities were occurring on the premises.32 The bookstore argued that the closure order, which incidentally burdened its First Amendment right to sell books, should be evaluated under the

The Supreme Court emphatically rejected this argument. Writing for the majority, Chief Justice Burger stated that the O'Brien test "has no relevance to a statute directed at imposing sanctions on nonexpressive activity, and the sexual activities carried on in this case manifest absolutely no element of protected expression".32 The Court concluded that "[b]ookselling in an establishment used for prostitution does not confer First Amendment coverage to defeat a valid statute aimed at penalizing and terminating illegal uses of premises".33 This holding represents a categorical denial by the Supreme Court that the conduct of prostitution contains any expressive element worthy of First Amendment consideration.

The legal conclusions regarding the speech and conduct of prostitution are mutually reinforcing. The finding that the conduct is non-expressive and illegal solidifies the conclusion that the speech proposing it is unprotected. Conversely, the fact that the speech constitutes a criminal solicitation reinforces the non-expressive, purely transactional nature of the conduct. This creates a closed legal loop from which prostitution cannot escape under current jurisprudence.
